Рецензент :                                                                          Черненко   Елена   Владимировна ,                                                                              специальный   корреспондент ,                                                                             Акционерное   общество  « КоммерсантЪ »   Санкт-Петербург 202 3 Аннотация .   Настоящее   исследование   посвящено   анализу   отношений   между   Европейским   союзом  и  Россией  в  контексте   киберпространства , с  особым   акцентом   на   их   влияние   на   сферу   кибербезопасности . В  данном   исследовании   рассматривается   влияние   социального   конструктивизма  ,   секьюритизации ,  нормативного   права    на   развитие   политики   кибербезопасности .  Исследование   имеет    практическое    значение   для   будущих   исследований  в  сфере   кибербезопасности  .  Признавая   динамичный   характер   кибервойны  и  развития    глобального   соперничества ,  заинтересованные   стороны   могут   укрепить   свой   потенциал   кибербезопасности  и  продвинуть   безопасную   цифровую   среду.Данная   диссертация   предлагает   научный   вклад  в  понимание   отношений   между   Европейским   Союзом  и  Россией  в  сфере   киберпространства . В  работе   подчеркивается   важность   теоретических   основ   для   понимания   кибербезопасности , и  предлагаются   эффективные   подходы  к  решению   проблем   кибербезопасности ,  что  в  конечном   итоге   приведет  к  созданию   более   безопасной   цифровой   среды . Ключевые   слова :   Россия ,  Европейский   Союз ,  киберпространство ,  кибербезопасность ,  борьба  с  кибертерроризмом ,  протоколы . Abstract.  The  present  study  is   focused  on  analyzing  the relations  between  the  European  Union and Russia in the  context  of cyberspace, with a  particular   emphasis  on  their   influence  on the domain of cybersecurity. The  present  study  examines  the impact of social  construction ,  securitization , normative power, power dynamics, and  hybrid  warfare on the  development  of cybersecurity policies and practices. The study  provides   theoretical   perspectives  for  comprehending  the  intricacies  of cyberspace. The study  holds   significant   practical   implications  for policymakers, security  experts , and  scholars .  It   outlines  the  provision  of  guidance   aimed   at  the  development  of  efficient  counter- cyberterrorism   protocols , frameworks, and  threat   assessments .  Through   acknowledgement  of the  dynamic   character  of cyber warfare and  cultivation  of global  cooperation , stakeholders can  augment   their  cybersecurity  capacities  and  advance  a secure  digital  milieu. In  its   entirety ,  this   dissertation   offers  a  scholarly   contribution  to the  comprehension  of the  relationship   between  the  European  Union and Russia in the  realm  of cyberspace.  This  highlights the  significance  of  theoretical  frameworks in  understanding  the  intricacies   involved  in cybersecurity and  presents   effective   approaches  to tackle cybersecurity  issues ,  ultimately   resulting  in a more secure  digital   environment . Key words:  Russia,  European  Union, cyberspace, cybersecurity, counter- cyberterrorism ,  protocols , frameworks,  threat   assessments . The  increasing   reliance  on  digital   technology  and the  interconnectedness  of cyberspace  have   resulted  in the  development  of new  forms  of warfare,  such   as  cyber warfare,  which   may  do  extensive   damage  to  vital   infrastructure ,  undermine  public trust, and  disrupt   democratic   processes . Cybersecurity relations  between  the  European  Union (EU) and Russia  have   been  tense and  sometimes   contentious . On  issues   such   as  internet governance, data privacy, and cybersecurity  threats , the  two  parties  have   had   opposing   views . In  its   early   years , Russia  was  slow to  embrace  the internet, and  its  government  had   little  control over online activities.  As  the internet  became   increasingly   vital  to  modern  life, Russia  began  to  see   it   as  a tool for  economic   growth  and  political  control.  Starting  in the  early  2000s, the Russian government  used   censorship  and  surveillance  to  maintain  control over the internet . In  contrast , the EU  has   always   been  a  fervent   advocate  of internet  freedom  and  digital  privacy. In  response  to  rising  risks  posed  by state- sponsored  hackers and  cybercriminals , the  European  Union (EU)  has   strengthened   its  cybersecurity  defenses . Many   cyberattacks  and hacking  attempts   have   strained   ties   between  the  European  Union and Russia in  recent   years . The EU  has   accused  Russia of a  number  of high- profile  cyber  attacks ,  including  the 2017  NotPetya  ransomware  epidemic ,  which   caused   significant   damage  to global businesses and  infrastructure . Russia  has   denied   involvement  in  these   attacks ,  but  the  European  Union  has   replied  with  economic   sanctions  and  other  steps to  deter  future  efforts . In  addition  to the  NotPetya   attack ,  other  high- profile  cyber  attacks   have   contributed  to the  aggravation  of  tensions   between  the EU and Russia. The 2015  attack  of the German  parliament ,  according  to the Russian hacker group APT28 ,  was  one of the  most   significant . The  attack   resulted  in the  theft  of sensitive  documents  and  seriously   hurt   Germany's   political  and  diplomatic  standing. Another   notable  event  was  the 2016  hack  of the  Democratic  National Committee (DNC) in the United States,  which   was   also   attributed  to APT28 . The  hack   was  part of a  larger   campaign  by the Russian government to  influence  the 2016  presidential   election  in the United States, and  it   resulted  in the  exposure  of  thousands  of key emails and  documents . The event  caused  a  substantial   diplomatic   rift   between  the United States and Russia and  has   been  the  subject  of  ongoing   investigations  and  political   discussion .  Notwithstanding   these   worries , the EU and Russia  have   continued  to  engage  in  conversations  over cybersecurity.  In 2018, the EU  accused  Russia of  initiating  a  cyberattack  on the Organization for the  Prohibition  of Chemical  Weapons  (OPCW) ,  which   was   investigating  the use of  chemical   weapons  in Syria. The  attack   aimed  to  disrupt  the  OPCW's   operations  and  weaken   its   credibility ;  it   was  part of a  bigger  Russian  campaign  of  disinformation  and propaganda. These  and  other   intrusions   have   escalated   distrust  and  hostility   between  the EU and Russia in cyberspace and cybersecurity.  While   Moscow   has   not   claimed   involvement  for a  number  of  these   assaults , the EU  has   responded  with  economic   sanctions  and  other   measures   intended  to  deter  future  assaults  and  hold  Russia  accountable  for  its   behavior . The parties'  ongoing   arguments   demonstrate  the  difficulty  of  regulating  cybersecurity in an  increasingly   interconnected  and  contentious   digital   environment . The Russian- Ukrainian  cyberspace and cybersecurity  conflict  of 2022  has   emphasized  the  vital   necessity  for  effective  counter- cyberterrorism   protocols , frameworks, and  threat   assessments . The  disagreements   have   been   characterized  by a surge in cyber  attacks ,  disinformation   campaigns , and  other   damaging  acts,  demonstrating  the  potential  for cyberspace to  heighten   tensions  and  exacerbate   conflicts . Relations  between  the EU and Russia  have   been   particularly   heated  in  this   context , with  each  side  accusing  the  other  of  cyberattacks  and  disinformation   campaigns . In  response  to the  annexation  of Crimea and the  ongoing   conflict  in  eastern  Ukraine, the EU  has   imposed   economic   sanctions  on Russia,  while   Moscow   has   taken  steps to  expand   its   influence  in the  region  and  weaken  the  EU's  standing in international  affairs . Cyberspace and cybersecurity  issues   have   added  a new  dimension  to an  already   complex   relationship , with  both  sides  using   digital   technology  to  further   their   strategic   objectives  and compromise the security of the  other . The impact of cyber  attacks  on  critical   infrastructure   has   also   become  a major  concern  for  many  governments,  since   interruptions  to  crucial  services  such   as   healthcare , power, and  transportation   may   have   disastrous   consequences  for public  safety  and  economic   stability .  This   has   resulted  in  increased  investment in cybersecurity by governments and the private  sector ,  as   well   as  the  development  of innovative cyber  defensive   technologies  and strategies. Yet , the  rapid  pace of  technological   advancement   also   causes  challenges for cybersecurity,  as  new  weaknesses  and  threats   constantly  emerge.  This   needs  a  dynamic  and  flexible  cybersecurity policy, in  which  businesses and governments must stay  vigilant  and  quickly   react  to  changing   circumstances . The use of cyberspace by non-state  actors ,  such   as   terrorist  groups and  criminal   organizations ,  has   added  a new  dimension  to the cybersecurity challenge.  These   perpetrators  are  often   highly   motivated  and  resourceful ,  using   advanced  ways and  technology  to evade  discovery  and  commit   hostile  activities.  This   requires  a  coordinated  and  determined   response  from the international community, in  which   states   exchange  information, formulate  agreed  policies, and  disrupt  the acts of  these   actors . Nonetheless , the  growing   importance  of data privacy and security  has   become  a major  problem  in cyberspace. Governments and businesses must take  precautions  to  protect  the  rising   amount  of personal and sensitive information  stored  and  processed  online  against   unauthorized  access and  theft .  This   requires  a  combination  of  technology   solutions ,  such   as   encryption  and access  limitations ,  as   well   as   proper  policies and practices for  managing  and  safeguarding  data. Cyberspace  is   becoming  a  crucial  arena for international security and  stability ,  needing   robust  cybersecurity  measures , international  cooperation , and a  dedication  to  protecting  data privacy and  democratic   norms . The  ongoing  cyberspace and cybersecurity  disputes   between  Russia and  Ukraine  demonstrate  the  gravity  of  these   concerns  and the  necessity  for a  coordinated  and  effective   response  from the international community.  Research   question  and  objectives This   master's   thesis   examines  EU-Russia relations in cyberspace and cybersecurity  amid  Russian- Ukrainian   hostilities  in 2022, with a focus on anti- cyberterrorism   protocols , frameworks, and  threats .  This  study  aims  to  respond  to the following  research   question :  during  the Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts  of 2022, to  what   extent   does  the concept of social  construction  impact the dynamics of EU-Russia relations  within  the  realm  of cyberspace?  Additionally ,  what   is  the impact of  securitization  on the  development  of cybersecurity policies and practices in the relations  between  the  European  Union and Russia? In  order  to  achieve   this  achievement,  there  are a  number  of tasks to  address :  -  Establish  the  theoretical  framework,  which   entails   outlining  the  structure   that   introduces  and  describes  the theory  that   elucidates  the  existence  of the  research   problem  under  investigation . -  Analyze  Russian  understanding  of “information security” concept,  through  a  chronological  review of  historical  events  that   influenced   it ,  then   analyze  the Russian  infosecurity  frameworks,  concerning  the concepts of  digital   sovereignty  and the information  regulations .  Subsequently ,  perform  a  critical   examination  of the Russian concept of Cyber  Sovereignty . -  Analyze  the EU  understanding  of “cybersecurity” concept,  through   critical  and  chronological   analysis   regarding  EU  digital  policy and EU  legal  framework for cybersecurity.  Ultimately , an  examination  of the  updated  global  digital  policy of the  European  Union  is   warranted . -  Conduct  a meta-cognitive  evaluation  of  two   distinct  frameworks and  comprehensions ,  which   were   previously   examined ,  utilizing  the  Constructivism  and  Securitization  theory- methodology , with the  aim  of  presenting  an innovative  comprehension  of the cybersecurity situation  between  the EU and Russia. -  Provide  an in- depth   analysis  of the cybersecurity  landscape   during  the 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts .  It   will   conduct  a  thorough   analysis  of the  historical   backdrop  of  cyberattacks   that   occurred   between  2014 and 2022,  as   well   as  the  specific   cyberattacks   that   transpired  in  February  2022. -  Utilize  the study and  analysis  of the 2017 " NotPetya " case study to gain a  deeper   comprehension  of the  tangible   effects  of cyber  attacks  in the  current  era. The  research   will   evaluate   significant  literature on EU-Russia  contacts  in cyberspace and cybersecurity,  as   well   as   methods , institutions, and  dangers   associated  with  fighting   cyberterrorism .  The  methodology  of the study  is   based  on   both  general  scientific  and special  research   methods ,  such   as : qualitative  research  design and data  collection   methods ,  such   as   document   analysis ,  expert  interviews, and case studies. The  research   will   evaluate  the  effectiveness  of  existing  counter- cyberterrorism  policies and frameworks in  handling  cyberspace and cybersecurity  threats   posed  by the 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflict . The  subject  of the  research   pertains  to the  examination  of the interactions  between  the  European  Union and Russia in the  realm  of cyberspace and cybersecurity,  specifically   during  the  period  of the 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts . The  research   encompasses  a  comprehensive   examination  of the  distinct   interpretations  and frameworks of the  two   components ,  coupled  with an  evaluation  of  the  maneuvers , techniques, and  undertakings   executed  by the  opposing   factions  in the  realm  of cyberspace and cybersecurity  amid  the  confrontations . The  object  of the study  is  the counter cyber- terrorism   protocols , frameworks, and  threats  in the  context  of the 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts . The study  will   examine  the  effectiveness  of  existing  counter cyber- terrorism   protocols  and frameworks in  addressing  the  threats   posed  by the  conflicts  in cyberspace and cybersecurity. The  research   will   also   analyze  the key challenges and  opportunities  for  improving   these   protocols  and frameworks. Literature and sources  analysis ,  regulatory   legal   documents  of the Russian Federation and the  European  Union  constitute  the official  discourse   that   pertains  to the policy of  both   entities  with  respect  to the  legal  frameworks and government  directives   that   govern  cyberspace. The  present   analysis   holds   significance  for future  investigations  in the  realm  of cybersecurity and the  assessment  of diverse  perspectives   pertaining  to cyberspace.  Regarding   the Russian   regulatory  framework, the  aforementioned   documents   underwent   analysis : (1)  Decree  of the  President  of Russian Federation from 05.12.2016 N646 of  May  12, 2016 “ Doctrine  of Information Security of the Russian Federation” ; (2) “INFORMATION SECURITY DOCTRINE OF THE RUSSIAN FEDERATION”  approved  by  President  of the Russian Federation Vladimir Putin on  September  9, 2000 ; (3) FEDERAL LAW NO. 149-FZ OF JULY 27, 2006 “ON INFORMATION, INFORMATIONAL TECHNOLOGIES AND THE PROTECTION OF INFORMATION”  Passed  by the State Duma on  July  8, 2006  Approved  by the  Federation  Council  on  July  14, 2006 ; (4)  Decree   No. 608767-7 “On  Amendments  to the Federal  Law  “On Communications” and the Federal  Law  “On Information, Information Technologies and Information  Protection ” ; (5) FEDERAL LAW NO. 126-FZ OF JULY 7, 2003 ON COMMUNICATIONS (with the  Amendments  and  Additions  of  December  23, 2003, August 22, November 2, 2004,  May  9, 2005,  February  2, March 3,  July  26, 27,  December  29, 2006)  Passed  by the State Duma on June 18, 2003  Approved  by the Federation  Council  on June 25, 2003 ; and  Decree  No. 113045-8 “On control over the activities of  persons  under  foreign   influence ” . When   it   comes  to  the EU  regulation : (1) “ Communication  from the  commission  to the   European   Parliament  and the  Council  on the Fifth Progress Report on the  implementation  of the EU Security Union Strategy”,  Brussels , 13.12.2022 ; (2)  Regulation  (EU) 2016/679 of the  European   Parliament  and of the  Council  of 27 April 2016 on the  protection  of  natural   persons  with  regard  to the processing of personal data and on the free  movement  of  such  data, and  repealing  Directive 95/46/EC (General Data  Protection   Regulation ) ; (3)  European   Parliament   resolution  of 15 June 2017 on online  platforms  and the  digital  single market (2016/2276(INI)) ; (4)  Regulation  (EU)  2019/881 of the  European   Parliament  and of the  Council  of 17 April 2019 on ENISA (the  European  Union Agency for Cybersecurity) and on information and  communications   technology  cybersecurity  certification  and  repealing   Regulation  (EU) No 526/2013 (Cybersecurity Act) ; (5) COMMUNICATION FROM THE COMMISSION TO THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT, THE COUNCIL, THE EUROPEAN ECONOMIC AND SOCIAL COMMITTEE AND THE COMMITTEE OF THE REGIONS A Digital Single Market Strategy for Europe; and the Official Journal of the  European  Union, C 493, 27  December  2022 . Scientific  publications ,  monographs , books and  articles  by Russian,  European  and international  authors   were   used  for the master  thesis .  Such  works  should  be  mentioned ,  since   they   analyzed  the concept of information security in Russia and cybersecurity in the  European  Union: B. Lilly, P.  Researcher , P. Rand, G. School, and J.  Cheravitch ,  The  Past ,  Present , and Future of  Russia’s  Cyber Strategy and  Forces  ; B. Buchanan,  The Cybersecurity Dilemma ; I.  Ciolan ,  DEFINING CYBERSECURITY AS THE SECURITY ISSUE OF THE TWENTY FIRST CENTURY. A CONSTRUCTIVIST APPROACH , and D. B. Parker,  Toward  a New Framework for Information Security? . The  content   analysis   was   conducted   also   utilizing   materials   sourced  from  European , Russian, and international media outlets. The media outlets  chosen  for the  purpose  of  this   analysis  include "TASS" , "NY Times" , and "The Guardian" . The  scientific   novelty  of  this  master  thesis   lies  in  its  focus on  analyzing  EU-Russia relations in cyberspace and cybersecurity  during  the 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts  with a  specific   emphasis  on counter- cyberterrorism   protocols , frameworks, and  threats .  This   is  a  relatively  new and  emerging  area of  research ,  given  the  increasing   reliance  on  technology  and  digital   platforms , and the  growing   importance  of cyber  operations  in  modern   conflicts . Significance  and  relevance  of the study The study  is   significant  for a  variety  of  reasons .  The  relevance  of  studying  EU-Russia relations in cyberspace and cybersecurity  during  the 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts   lies  in the  growing   importance  of cyber  operations  in  modern   conflicts  and the  potential   implications  for international relations and global security.  With  increasing   reliance  on  technology  and  digital   platforms  in  various   sectors , cybersecurity  has   become  a  critical   concern  for  nations   worldwide . The EU-Russia relations in cyberspace can  have   implications   not   only  for the  two   entities   but   also  for global cybersecurity. The 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts  can  provide  insights  into   how  cybersecurity  threats  are  evolving , and  how   nations  are  responding  to  these   threats .  Moreover , the EU and Russia  have  a  complex   relationship   that   has   been   strained  by  various   political  and  economic   disputes ,  including  cybersecurity  concerns .  As  the EU and Russia continue to  engage  in a range of activities in cyberspace,  such   as   espionage , propaganda, and  disinformation   campaigns ,  it   is   crucial  to study  how   these  activities  affect  the overall state of EU-Russia relations and  what  steps can be  taken  to  manage   potential  risks and  threats . The  ongoing   conflicts   between  Russia and Ukraine  have   significant   geopolitical   implications  for Europe and  beyond . The EU  has  a  stake  in  ensuring   stability  in  its   neighborhood  and  safeguarding   its  security  interests .  Understanding  the dynamics of EU-Russia relations in cyberspace  during  the  conflicts  can  provide  insights  into  the  broader   political   implications  of the situation. The 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts   highlighted  the  increasing  use of  cyberattacks   as  a tool of  statecraft , with  both  sides  engaging  in  various   forms  of cyber  operations .  Understanding  the nature of  these   attacks ,  their   motivations , and  their   potential   consequences   is   essential  to  developing   effective   countermeasures  and  protocols  to  prevent   similar   incidents  in the future.   In  addition  to  political   implications , cybersecurity  threats  are  not  limited to state  actors   but   also  include non-state  actors   such   as   terrorist   organizations .  Understanding   how  counter- terrorism   protocols  and frameworks are  being   employed  by the EU and Russia in cyberspace  during  the  conflicts  can  provide  insights  into   how   nations  are  responding  to  these   threats .  Overall,  studying  EU-Russia relations in cyberspace and cybersecurity  during  the 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts  can help policymakers, security  experts , and  scholars   better   understand  the  evolving  nature of cyber warfare and  its  impact on international security and  diplomacy .  It  can  also   contribute  to the  academic   understanding  of cybersecurity, international relations, and counter- terrorism ,  providing   empirical   evidence  of  how   nations  are  responding  to cybersecurity  threats  in real-time and  bridging  the gap  between  theory and practice . CHAPTER 1 - THEORETICAL CHAPTER Cyberspace  has   profoundly   altered  the way  states   interact  with one  another  and  added  a  previously   nonexistent   dimension  to international relations. Cyberspace  has   enabled   states  to project power and  influence   across   borders   without   physically  crossing  them .  This   has   resulted  in the  evolution  of new  forms  of  statecraft  and  strategic   thought .  Moreover , cyberspace  has   posed  new challenges to  traditional   notions  of state  sovereignty  and  territorial   integrity . Cyberspace  is   inherently   borderless , making  it   challenging  for  states  to  exert  control over  it .  As   states   attempt  to  reconcile  the  need  for security with the desire for  openness  and  innovation ,  this   has  led to the  development  of new  norms  and rules of  behavior  in cyberspace. Cyberspace  has   also   become  an  essential  arena for international  conflict  and  competition . States  engage  in a  variety  of activities in cyberspace,  such   as   espionage ,  sabotage , and  intellectual   property   theft .  Consequently , new  forms  of  cyberwarfare  and cybersecurity  have   emerged . Cyberspace  has   had  a  substantial  impact on international relations and  has   presented   states  with new challenges and  opportunities .  Understanding  the dynamics of cyberspace  is   crucial  for  both  policymakers and  academics . The school of  thought   known   as   constructivism   is   commonly   regarded   as  a  suitable  framework for  examining  the  subject  of EU-Russia relations in the  context  of cyberspace and cybersecurity.  However , alternative schools of  thought ,  including   realism ,  neoliberalism , and  Marxism ,  may   not   possess  the  same   level  of  direct   applicability . Realism   is  a  theoretical  framework  that  places  significant   emphasis  on power dynamics and the  pursuit  of national  interests . The  theoretical  framework  regards   nation-states   as  the  principal  agents in the  realm  of global  affairs , and places  significant   emphasis  on the dynamics of  rivalry  and security  predicaments .  Realism ,  as  a  theoretical  framework,  tends  to  prioritize  the  recognition  of  cybersecurity's   significance  and the  potential  for  cyberterrorism .  However ,  it   tends  to  disregard  the  role  of non-state  actors  and the impact of  norms ,  ideas , and social  constructs   that   shape  international relations.  When   examining  the  relationship   between  the  European  Union and Russia in the  realm  of cyberspace,  it   may  be  insufficient  to  solely   consider  power dynamics and  tangible   resources   as   it   fails  to  fully   encompass  the intricate nature of the  developing   digital   environment . Neoliberalism ,  also   referred  to  as  liberal  institutionalism ,  prioritizes  the  values  of  cooperation , institutions, and  interdependence   among   states . The  statement  underscores the  capacity  of global  organizations  to  effectively  handle  disputes  and  promote  collaborative  efforts .  Notwithstanding , the  neoliberal   perspective   may   fail  to  consider  the  influence  of  norms ,  identity , and social  construction  on the dynamics of EU-Russia interactions in the  realm  of cyberspace.  Effective  cybersecurity and counter cyber- terrorism   endeavors  necessitate  not   only   institutional   collaboration   but   also  a  comprehension  of the  fundamental   convictions ,  principles , and  concerns  of the parties  concerned . Moreover ,  Marxism  places  emphasis  on the  economic  and class dynamics  within  the  realm  of international relations, with a  specific  focus on the  function  of  capitalism  and the act of exploitation.  Although  the  analysis   may   provide   significant   perspectives  on the  wider   geopolitical  and  economic   dimensions  of EU-Russia relations,  it   may   not   specifically  tackle the  complexities  of cyberspace and cybersecurity. The  Marxist   perspective   exhibits  a  tendency  to  accord   greater   significance  to  socioeconomic   factors  and  systemic   structures ,  which   may   not   comprehensively   encapsulate  the intricate nature of cyber  threats ,  norms , and governance  within  the framework of EU-Russia relations. In  contrast , the  constructivist   approach   is   deemed  more appropriate for  analyzing  EU-Russia relations in cyberspace and cybersecurity.  This   is   because   it  places  emphasis  on the  significance  of  ideas ,  norms , and social interactions in shaping international relations. The  theoretical  framework of  constructivism   delves   into  the cognitive  processes  of  individuals  in  their   understanding  and  interpretation  of cyberspace, cybersecurity, and cyber  threats .  It   also   examines  the impact of  these   perceptions  on  their  decision-making and policy  formulation . The  acknowledgement  of the  significance  of social  norms ,  collective   comprehension , and the establishment of  identity   is   pertinent  to  comprehending  the  developing  dynamics of the  European  Union and  Russia's   relationship  in the  digital   realm . 1.1  Theoretical  framework of  Constructivism Constructivism   is  a  theoretical   approach  in the field of International Relations (IR)  that   emphasizes  the  role  of social  norms ,  ideas , and  identities  in shaping global  politics .  It   views  international relations  as  a social  construct   rather   than  a  purely   material  or  objective   phenomenon .  According  to  constructivists , the  meanings  and  identities   that   states  and  other   actors   ascribe  to international  politics  are  critical  in  determining   their   behavior  and interactions. Alexander  Wendt , a  leading   scholar  in  constructivist  IR theory,  argues   that  the social  structure  of international relations  is   constituted  by the  ideas  and  identities  of  actors   rather   than   material  power alone.  Wendt's   distinction   between   anarchy   as  a  material   condition  versus  anarchy   as  a social  construct   illustrates  the  fundamental   constructivist   assumption   that  social practices and  ideas ,  rather   than   objective   material  realities,  shape  international relations. Another   influential   constructivist   scholar   is  Nicholas  Onuf ,  who   emphasizes  the  role  of  language  and  communication  in the  creation  of international  norms  and  identities .  Onuf   argues   that  the way  we  talk  about  international  politics   constructs  and  reinforces   particular   identities  and  norms ,  which  in turn  shape  the  behavior  of  states  and  other   actors . Other   notable   constructivist   theorists  include Martha  Finnemore ,  who   has   studied  the  role  of  norms  and  ideas  in shaping state  behavior  in  areas   such   as   humanitarian   intervention  and  nuclear   proliferation , and Emanuel Adler,  who   has   explored  the  role  of  identity  and culture in shaping international relations. Finnemore   argues   that  international  norms ,  such   as   those   related  to human  rights  or  environmental   protection , are  not   simply   imposed  on  states ,  but  are  constructed   through  a  process  of  socialization .  She   suggests   that  the  behavior  of  states  can be  influenced  by the  norms  to  which   they   subscribe , and  that   norms  can  change  over time  as  a  result  of social interactions  between   states . In the  context  of the study of EU-Russia relations in cyberspace and cybersecurity  during  the 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts , a  Constructivist  framework  could  be  useful  in  analyzing   how  the social  constructions  of cybersecurity and  cyberterrorism   shape  the  behavior  of EU and Russian  actors . The study  could   examine   how  the  norms  and  identities   associated  with cyber  operations ,  such   as  hacking and  disinformation   campaigns ,  have   developed  and  evolved  over time, and  how   these   constructions   have   influenced  EU and Russian  approaches  to cybersecurity and  counterterrorism .  Additionally , the study  could   explore   how  EU and Russian  actors   construct   their   respective   identities  in the  context  of cyber  conflict  and  how   these   identities   shape   their   behavior  in cyberspace. By  utilizing  the insights of  Constructivism ,  this  study  could   provide  a more  nuanced   understanding  of the dynamics of EU-Russia relations in cyberspace and cybersecurity  during  the 2022 Russian- Ukrainian   conflicts . 1.2 Copenaghen School and  Securitization The  constructivist   perspective   proposes   that  the dynamics of international relations are  shaped  by a  socially  and  historically   constructed  reality  where  the  significance  of human nature or power relations  is   secondary ,  if   not   negligible . The  fundamental  premise of  constructivism   posits   that  the  prevailing   political   cultures   during  a  particular  era are  responsible  for shaping the global reality in  which   states   exist . The  absence  of a  governing  authority in the international system  is  a  manifestation  of  how   states   perceive  the  character  of  their  interactions. . Furthermore ,  it   is   possible  to  affirm   that  the theory of  Constructivism   posits  a  reevaluation  of international relations  as  a  distinct   form  of human relations on a global scale.  This   approach   posits   that  the  formation  of  identities  and  interests   occurs   concurrently  with the  process  of interaction.  Constructivist   scholars   begin   their   analysis  by  comprehending  the  individual  and social interactions, and  subsequently   broaden   their  scope to  encompass  the  institutional  and global  levels . The  multitude  of  consequences   that   have   arisen  from  current  security practices  have  led to a shift in the  logic  and nature of security,  resulting  in the  emergence  of a new security  paradigm .  Historically , the  responsibility  of  ensuring  national security  has   been   attributed  to the government and  has   relied  on  foreign  policies,  military  capabilities, and the intelligence community.  However ,  contemporary   perspectives  on  critical   infrastructure   posit   that   it   represents  a collaborative  responsibility ,  wherein  the government alone  is   insufficient  in  providing  the requisite  level  of security .  Therefore ,  it   is   equally  imperative to  maintain  the  involvement  of private,  local , or  individual   entities  in  ensuring  network security,  as   it   is  for national or international  endeavors  to  safeguard  the  digital   landscape . Thus , a  novel  framework  has   been   introduced   wherein  security  is   considered  a speech act. The  Copenhagen  School  is   credited  with the  development  of the theory of  securitization .  Indeed   it   refers  to the idea  that  a  securitizing   actor   might   assert  the authority to take  extraordinary   measures  to  protect  the life of a  referent   object  by  asserting   that  the  object's   existence   is  in  danger . The  aforementioned   matter   is   subsequently   transitioned  from the domain of  conventional   politics  to the domain of  necessary   politics ,  wherein   it  can be  promptly   addressed   without   usual  ( demographic )  protocols  and  procedures  of policy making. In the  realm  of security, the  term  no  longer   possesses  a  fixed   definition  and  is   subject  to the  interpretation  of the  actor   who   seeks  to  securitize   it . The concept of security,  as   perceived  in  this   manner ,  is  a product of social  construction ,  whereby  the  interpretation  of security  is   reliant   upon   its   application . It   is   within   this   particular  framework  that  one  may   assert   that  Cybersecurity  threats   have   altered  the  societal  frameworks of security and  conflicts ,  including   their   norms  and  regulations   regarding   participation . The social  hierarchy  of  violence  in the  virtual   realm   is   indistinct , and the  differentiation   between  non- combatants  and  combatants   is   ambiguous . Therefore , an  analysis  of the  existing  cybersecurity policy and  its   associated  risks  through  a  constructivist   lens   presents  a  compelling   opportunity  to gain  deeper  insights  into  the  phenomena  and  behaviors   within  the cyberspace domain. 1.3  Understanding  the Cyberspace From a  broader  and more  generic   perspective , cybersecurity can be  defined   as  the  safeguarding  of computer systems and data from  harm ,  theft , and  unauthorized  access . Security  measures   pertaining  to computer information and access deal with the following  four   main   threats . The  various   forms  of computer- related   offenses  include the  illicit   acquisition  of information,  such   as   confidential   military  intelligence from government computer systems, the  intentional   destruction  of data  through  the use of computer  viruses ,  fraudulent  activities  such   as  the  misappropriation  of funds by bank  employees , and the  infringement  of privacy  rights ,  such   as  the  unauthorized  access of  protected  personal  financial  or  medical  data from a  vast  database.  The  significance  of cybersecurity  has   been  on the rise  since  the late 1960s,  coinciding  with the  introduction  of  modems . The  issue   worsened   as  a  result  of the 1980s'  widespread  adoption of personal computers,  which  made  it   easier  for hackers to gain  unauthorized  access to  significant  computer networks. The  proliferation  of the Internet  during  the  latter  part of the 20th  century  and  the  early   years  of the 21st  century   has  led to a  significant   increase  in the  importance  of computer security . The  objective  of  enhancing  security  measures   is  to mitigate  potential   threats ,  however , the  continuous   evolution  of computer crime techniques  presents   persistent  risks.  Prior  to 2007, national policy makers  did   not   prioritize  cyber security,  resulting  in  debates   that   were   largely   theoretical   rather   than   grounded  in  empirical   evidence . In  recent   years , cybersecurity  has   emerged   as  a  crucial   subject   that   has   garnered  the  interest  of  various  stakeholders  such   as  national policymakers, IT  specialists ,  diplomats , intelligence  agencies , and  military   officials . The  objective   is  to  identify   effective   measures  to counter the risks  posed  by cyberspace.  Despite   being  an  established  field, the  prevalence  of cyber activities  has   grown   significantly , with  corresponding   advancements  in  their   intricacy  and  sophistication . From the IR theory  perspective , the  comprehension  of cyberspace  is   not  a one- sided   endeavor .  Within  the  scientific   discourse , cyberspace  is   commonly   linked   solely  with the Internet ,  although   its   boundaries   extend   beyond  the  confines  of the Internet  as  a  whole .  Several   theoretical  frameworks  have   been   developed  to  comprehend  the  complexities  of cyberspace.  Conforming  to the  findings  of F. Kramer,  there   existed  a maximum of 28  interpretations  of the  term  "cyberspace" .  Among  IR  scholars   who   employ  the  constructivist   approach  to  analyze  cyberspace,  it   is   feasible  to  identify  the idea of J. Eriksson and G. Giacomello ,  which   claim   that  the Internet  functions   as   a  mechanism  for  disseminating  information  while   concurrently   possessing  a  distinct   character   that   has   undergone   continual   transformation .  It   is   crucial  to  realize   that  the culture and  identity  of the online  space   frequently   influence  the  dissemination  of information and the  behavior  of  numerous  users on the Internet. Cyberspace  is  a  novel   phenomenon   that  demands a  reevaluation  of interactions  among   states , non-state  actors , and  other   states   through  a  constructivist   lens , in  order  to  align  with the  contemporary  era of the Digital Age . 1.4 Russia and EU  Understanding  of Cybersecurity The  varying   interpretations  of the  cybersphere , cybersecurity, information  sphere , and  infosecurity  concepts  between  Western  nations  ( such   as  EU  member   states ) and  other  countries ( such   as  Russia)  have   resulted  in  instability  in global  politics . The  absence  of a  commonly   agreed   upon  framework,  consisting  of  fundamental   values  and standards,  impedes  the  advancement  of  synchronized   worldwide  cyberspace and cybersecurity  measures . The Russian Federation  does   not   possess  a  specific  and explicit  characterization  of the  virtual  domain  commonly   referred  to  as  cyberspace.  Considerable  literature in Russian  exists   regarding  the Russian  government's   definition  of the "information  sphere ,"  encompassing   both  the technical  processes   within  cyberspace and  its   content . The  aforementioned   expression   is   utilized  and  advocated  by  governmental   representatives  of Russia in  both   domestic  and international  spheres  of policy. The  European  Union  employs  the  term  "cyberspace" in  its   discourse ,  however ,  it   lacks  a precise and  unambiguous   definition  of the  term  in  its  official  documentation . The  typical  framework of  legal   provisions  centers on cyberspace  as  a  worldwide   realm   that   encompasses  a  mutually   reliant  system of information  infrastructures ,  comprising  computer systems,  telecommunication  networks, and the Internet.  With  reference  to the  aforementioned  theory,  as   posited  by the  Copenhagen  school of  thought , cybersecurity  is  the  outcome  of the  amalgamation  of  technology ,  protocols , and  customary  practices. The study centers on the  utilization  of diverse  actors '  representations  of risk to  shape  or alter  societal , private, commercial, and  governmental   perceptions  of security  within   specific  public domains .  Hence , the  contrast   between  the  European   methodology   towards  the cyberspace and the Russian  methodology   towards  the information  sphere   is   not   incongruous  and can be  subjected  to comparative  analysis . CHAPTER 2 - “Information Security”: Russian  understanding  of cyberspace The  notion  of security  has   experienced   notable   changes  in the  constantly   evolving   digital   realm ,  particularly  in relation to information and cyberspace. The  growing   significance  of information and  communication   technologies  in  modern  societies  has   prompted   states  and international  actors  to  acknowledge  the  necessity  of  safeguarding   their   interests , assets, and  citizens   within   this   realm . The  present   chapter   examines  the  unique   viewpoints  of Russia and the  European  Union (EU) with  regard  to information security and cybersecurity.  Additionally ,  it   investigates  the  evolving  nature of EU-Russia relations in the  realm  of cyberspace. The  notion  of " infosecurity " in Russia  is   firmly   entrenched  in the  nation's   historical  background and  its  national security  doctrine . The concept  pertains  to  safeguarding  the  autonomy  of the state, the  preservation  of  its   geographical   boundaries , and the  sustenance  of  domestic   harmony ,  all   within  the  purview  of information and  communication   technologies . The Russian government places  significant   emphasis  on the  regulation  and  supervision  of information  dissemination ,  prioritizing   efforts  to  combat   external   manipulation  and  prevent   internal   opposition . The Russian  perspective  on information security  is   closely   linked  to concepts of state authority,  strategic   communication , and the  regulation  of information for  both   protective  and aggressive  objectives . In  contrast , the  European   Union's   notion  of "cybersecurity"  is   informed  by a  multifaceted  framework  that   incorporates   technological  and  legal   dimensions . The  European  Union places  significant   emphasis  on  safeguarding   critical   infrastructures ,  preserving  the privacy of  its   citizens , and  cultivating  trust and  resilience  in  digital  systems .  These   efforts  are  aimed   at   promoting  a secure and  trustworthy  cyberspace. The  European   Union's   approach  to cybersecurity  entails   augmenting   collaboration   among   its   constituent   states ,  formulating   all-encompassing   legal   structures ,  advocating  for global standards, and  allocating   resources   towards   research  and  development  to  address   nascent  risks in the  digital  domain . The  realm  of cyberspace  has   emerged   as  a domain  where  the interactions  between  the  European  Union and Russia  have   encountered   notable   intricacies  and  dynamic   developments . The cyber domain  relationship   between  the  European  Union (EU) and Russia  is   typified  by a blend of  collaboration ,  rivalry , and  geopolitical  strains . The  European  Union and Russia are  involved  in  several   initiatives  and  dialogues   concerning  cyber- related   matters ,  including  confidence-building  measures  and  capacity -building  programs .  However , due to  conflicting   interests  and  differing   perceptions  of information security,  tensions   arised . The  relationship   between  the  European  Union and Russia in cyberspace  may  be  further   complicated  by  occurrences  of cyber  espionage ,  disinformation   campaigns , and  purported   cyberattacks   that  are  believed  to  have   been   sponsored   by the state. In light of the  swift   evolution  of the  worldwide   digital   terrain ,  it   is   crucial  to gain a  comprehensive   understanding  of the Russian  notion  of " infosecurity ," the  European   Union's   notion  of "cybersecurity," and the  complex   interplay   that   influences  the  relationship   between  the EU and Russia in the cyber  sphere .  The  intent  of  this   chapter   is  to  examine  the Russian  comprehension  of the  notion  of "information security"  through  a  systematic   examination  of  past   occurrences   that   have   influenced   it . The  present  study  conducts  a  thorough   analysis  of the information security frameworks in Russia, with a  particular   emphasis  on the  notions  of  digital   sovereignty  and information  regulation . The  subsequent   chapter   provides  a  critical   analysis  of the Russian  notion  of "Cyber  Sovereignty " and  its   potential  impact on the  formation  of  worldwide  information security. 2.1  Infosecurity   According  to the Russian  Perspective Russia's   utilization  of  terminology   pertaining  to cyber- sovereignty ,  infosecurity ,  is  indicative of  its   unique   viewpoint  and  holds   significant   influence  in  molding  the  dialogue  and  comprehension  of cyber- related   notions   within  the Russian  context . The  notion  of information  dominance   holds  a  crucial  position in the Russian strategy of cyber- sovereignty ,  especially   since  the turn of the millennium in the  early  2000s . The  aforementioned   terminology  highlights the  crucial   importance  of information in  molding   perceptions ,  fabricating   narratives , and  impacting   pivotal  decision-making  procedures  on  both  a national and global scale .  Furthermore ,  this   type  of  terminology  highlights the  acknowledgement   that  information  has  the  potential  to serve  as  a  powerful   instrument  in  attaining   geopolitical  goals and  safeguarding  national  interests   within  the  digital  domain. The Russian government places  emphasis  on the  digital   realm   as  a "information  sphere ",  that   is   perceived  to  encompass  a  wider  scope  than  the Western  notion  of the "cyber domain". The 2016  Doctrine  of Information Security in Russia  provides  an  outline  of the information  sphere ,  describing   it   such   as  a “ combination ” of  several  “ information ”  aspects . The  aforementioned   passage , ( referring  to the point I of the  Doctrine )   pertains  to the  amalgamation  of  various   components   such   as  information,  informatisation   objects , information systems, and websites,  which  are  interconnected   through  the information and  telecommunications  network of the Internet.  This  network  encompasses   communication  networks, information  technologies ,  entities   responsible  for  generating  and processing information, and  those   involved  in the  development  and  utilization  of  such   technologies .  Additionally ,  it   encompasses   mechanisms   that   regulate  social relations  within   this   sphere ,  as   well   as   measures  to  ensure  information security. From a  constructivist   standpoint , the Russian  stance  on cyber- sovereignty  can be  interpreted   as   being   based  on the  notion  of information  supremacy    ( referring  to the point II and IV of the  Doctrine ) .  This   viewpoint   posits   that  the control of information  dissemination  and  substance   is  of  paramount   importance  in  upholding  the  autonomy  of the Russian  nation -state.  Moreover , the lexicon  utilized  in the Russian  methodology   conveys  the  notion   that  data  is   not   solely  a  valuable   resource ,  but   also  a  possible   weakness : “ the information  threat  to the Russian Federation  refers  to a  collection  of actions and  factors   that  pose a risk of  harming  national  interests   within  the information  sphere ” ( referring  to the point 2.B of the I part) and more  generally  “ the  main  information  threats  and the state of information security ” ( referring  to the III part of the  Doctrine ). The  Copenhagen   School's   securitization  framework highlights the  importance  of  implementing  strong  countermeasures  to  protect   against   potential  cyber  threats   that   may  pose a risk to national security or  destabilize  the Russian government. The  notion  of information  dominance   is   utilized  in the Russian  context  to underscore the  necessity  of  establishing  authority over information  dissemination   as  a strategy to  safeguard   against   external   interventions , propaganda  initiatives , and  perceived   efforts  to  destabilize  the country. The Russian government  employs  the strategy of  presenting  information control  as  a security  concern  in  order  to validate and  rationalize  the  acquisition  of power over information  channels .  This   approach   serves  to  strengthen  the  belief   that   such  control  is   essential  for  preserving  the  soundness  and  constancy  of the state. Adopting  a  constructivist   viewpoint ,  it  can be  argued   that  Russia  regards  the information  sphere   as  a  geopolitical   construct .  This   is   evidenced  by the  country's   tendency  to  ascribe   importance  to  its   domestic  information  space  by linking  it  to the  territorial   boundaries  of the  nation . The  theoretical  framework of  constructivism   emphasizes   that  the concept of a national  portion  of the Internet  is   not   inherently   static ,  but   rather  a product of social  construction   that   is   shaped  by a range of  processes  and interactions. Russia  has   established  a framework of  principles  and practices to  operationalize  the concept of  digital   sovereignty .  This  framework  serves   as  an  infrastructure  to demarcate  digital   boundaries  and  promote  the  attainment  of  functional   digital   sovereignty . 2.1  Russia's  Information Security:  Historical   Influences  and Cyber- Sovereignty The Russian  approach  to information security  has   been   influenced  by a  confluence  of  historical  events, the  dynamic   technological   environment , and the  perceived   prospects  and  obstacles   that   have   emerged  in the  digital  era . Russia  has   acknowledged  the  profound   influence  of information  technologies  and the  increasing   importance  of cyberspace in shaping  worldwide  events .  Consequently , the information security strategy of Russia  has   undergone  a  transformation  to  effectively  tackle the intricate dynamics of the  digital  domain and  safeguard  the  country's  national  interests . The  development  of the Russian  infosecurity   approach   has   been   significantly   influenced  by  historical   experiences . The  employment  of information warfare and  disinformation   campaigns   as  tools of  statecraft   during  the  Cold  War era  has   had  a lasting impact on the  experiences  of the  Soviet Union .  Historical   precedents   have   fostered  the  belief   that  information can be  employed   as  a tool to  achieve   strategic   objectives ,  shape   narratives , and  influence  decision-making  processes . The insights  gained  from  this   historical   period   have   influenced   Russia's  strategy for  managing  information and the  acknowledgment  of information  superiority   as  a  crucial   aspect  of  safeguarding  national security. Over the  past  decade, with  regards  to the  previously   mentioned  " historical  events,"  it   is   practicable  to  identify   three   primary   categories : Cyberattacks  and Cyber Warfare Color  Revolutions Arab  Spring (1)  Several  cyber  incidents   that   have   targeted  Russia,  including  the  Stuxnet  worm ,  Flame  malware , and  purported  cyber  operations   ascribed  to Western  nations ,  have   had  a  noteworthy   influence  on the  country's   stance   towards  information security. The  aforementioned   attacks   have   brought  to light the  susceptibility  of Russian information systems,  thereby   emphasizing  the  necessity  for strong  defensive   measures  and  countermeasures . The  uprisings ,  which   were  made  possible  by the internet and social media,  caused   concern  in  Moscow ,  leading  to a  reevaluation  of the  potential  hazards  linked  to the  unrestricted   dissemination  of information and the  ability  of  digital   platforms  to  shape   political  dynamics.  (3)  Furthermore , the  Arab  Spring  revolts  in the Middle East and North Africa area  underscored  the  influential   function  of social media in  galvanizing   widespread   demonstrations  and  instigating   political   transformations . Russia  has   acknowledged  the  significance  of information and  communication   technologies  ( ICTs )  as   instruments  for  political   mobilization  and  destabilization , and  has   consequently   recognized  the  necessity  to  adjust  to  this   evolving   digital   terrain . The  Arab  Spring  occurrences   acted   as  a  stimulus  for the Russian government,  prompting   them  to  recognize  the  significance  of information  propagation  and the imperative of  regulating  the  presentation  and  discussion  of  ideas   within   their   own   nation . The reaction of Russia to  these  events  is  indicative of  its   conviction  in the  necessity  of  strengthening   its   defenses   against  cyber hazards and  mitigating  the  perils  of  external   intervention  via information  manipulation . Russia  has   positioned  information security  as  a  crucial  component of  its  national security and  sovereignty  by  implementing   measures  to secure the  digital   realm .  Drawing   upon  the  constructivist   perspective  of  intersubjectivity , the Russian Federation  has   formulated  a  comprehensive   doctrine  for information warfare  aimed   at   safeguarding   its   interests  and  countering   external   influence .  This   entails   utilizing  a blend of government- managed  media, groups of internet users  who  post  inflammatory   comments  to  provoke   others ,  organizations   that   engage  in  unauthorized  access to computer systems, and  efforts  to disseminate  biased  or  misleading  information with the  aim  of  influencing  public opinion and  regulating  the  dissemination  of news.  These   measures  are  deemed   crucial  for  upholding   Russia's   stability  and  preserving   its   values  and  identity  in  response  to  perceived  challenges.  Russia's   securitization  of information in the  digital  domain  is  indicative of a social  construct  of security.  Through   discursive  practices and the  mobilization  of  actors , the state  has   effectively   framed  information security  as  a  threat  to  its   very   existence . Russia  employs  a survival- based  narrative to  rationalize   its  assertive  stance  and validate  its   endeavors  to  establish   dominance  in the  realm  of information security . The  swift   progressions  in  technology  and the  increasing   interconnectivity  of the  digital   sphere   have   prompted  Russia to  modify   its  information security strategy . The  growing   dependence  on  digital   platforms ,  communication  networks, and information  technologies   has   given  rise to a range of  prospects  and  obstacles . The Russian government  has   acknowledged  the  necessity  of  safeguarding   crucial  information  infrastructure ,  reducing  cyber risks, and  resolving   weaknesses  in the  digital  domain. The Russian  approach  to information security  has   integrated   various   measures   such   as   regulatory  frameworks,  surveillance  capabilities, and the  creation  of  specialized  government  entities   that  focus on cyber defense and information control.  This   has   resulted  in the  development  of the Russian  infosecurity   approach . The information security strategy in Russia  can be  interpreted   as  a  consequence  of the social  construction  of  sovereignty  and the  importance   placed  on  retaining  authority over the flow of information in the  digital  domain. The Russian government  views   external   influences  and  interference  in cyberspace  as  a challenge to  its   sovereignty ,  which   informs   its   efforts  to  protect  national  interests  and  uphold   domestic   stability . The  process  of  securitization  can be  observed  in  Russia's  information security strategy,  wherein  the government frames the  matter  of information security  as  a national security  issue . The Russian government  seeks  to  justify   its   endeavors  to  safeguard  national  interests ,  uphold   internal   stability , and  establish  authority over the  dissemination  of information in the  digital   sphere  by  means  of  securitizing  information security.  Furthemore ,  it   employs  ( as   showed   previously  from the  document  “ On  approval  of the Information Security  Doctrine  of the Russian Federation ” ) in the  justification  of  safeguarding  the national information  space  to  legitimize   its   measures   aimed   at   protecting   against  and  countering   external   influences  and  interference  in cyberspace,  which   it   perceives   as  a challenge to  its   sovereignty . The  process  of  securitization   serves  to  bolster  the  government's   jurisdiction  and  enhance   its   regulation  of information  dissemination ,  thereby   facilitating  the  development  of a  unique  cybersecurity  environment . The  perceived  cyber  threats  are a  contributing   factor  to the  implementation  of  legal  frameworks and policies  that  are  designed  to  improve   governmental   supervision  of data and information.  Russia's   attempts  to  strengthen   its  grip on  digital   communication   channels  and  assert   its  control over the flow of information are  exemplified  by the  Law  on Information, Information Technologies, and Information  Protection ,  as   well   as  the  Law  on  Sovereign  Internet. 2.2  Russia's  Cybersecurity Framework:  Protecting  Digital  Sovereignty  and Balancing Information  Regulation Russia  has   implemented  a set of cybersecurity  laws  and  regulations  to  safeguard   its   critical   infrastructure  and  combat  cyber  threats ,  recognizing  the  significance  of  protecting  national  interests  and  securing  the  digital   realm . The  Law  on Information, Information Technologies, and Information  Protection   is  a  pivotal  legislative  instrument   that   underpins   Russia's  cybersecurity framework .  These   legislations   demonstrate  the  nation's   dedication  to  upholding  cyber  resilience ,  augmenting   governmental  authority over the  dissemination  of information, and  safeguarding  the  digital   sovereignty  of the Russian state.  Furthermore ,  they  delineate the  lawful   structure  for  safeguarding  information, the  obligations  of  governmental   entities  and establishments, and the actions  necessary  to  forestall ,  identify , and  counteract  cyber  occurrences . The  legislation   additionally  sets  forth   stipulations   regarding  data  localization ,  mandating   that   entities  must  retain  the personal data of Russian  nationals  on servers  situated   within  the  borders  of Russia.  This   measure   facilitates   increased   regulation  and  supervision  of the  dissemination  of information. The  Law  on  Sovereign  Internet   is  a  notable   legislation   that   confers   upon  Russian  authorities  the  ability  to  regulate  and  govern  the  dissemination  of data  across  the  nation's  networks. The  objective  of the  legislation   is  to  guarantee  the  consistent   functionality  of the Russian  sector  of the cyberspace in the  occurrence  of  potential   external  hazards or  disturbances . The government  is   granted  the authority to create a  centralized  system for filtering internet  traffic ,  which  can  increase   their  control over the  dissemination  of information  .  This   may   also  lead to the  possibility  of  restricting  access to  specific  websites or online  content   that   is   perceived   as  a risk to national security.  Furthermore , Russia  has   implemented   measures  to  govern  the  operations  of internet service providers ( ISPs ) and  telecommunications   operators , in  addition  to the  aforementioned   law .  The Federal  Law  on Communications   delineates  the duties and liabilities of Internet Service Providers ( ISPs ) and  telecommunication   operators  with  regard  to  safeguarding  the  integrity  of  their  networks and  preserving  the  confidentiality  of user information .  This   statute   mandates   that  Internet Service Providers ( ISPs ) collaborate with state  entities  to  guarantee  the  protection  and  soundness  of the  digital  framework,  which   encompasses   furnishing   technological   aid  for investigative  objectives . Moreover , the Russian government  has   instituted   dedicated   governmental   entities  to  supervise   endeavors   related  to cybersecurity. The  coordination  of cybersecurity activities, policy  development , and  implementation  of  measures  to  safeguard   critical  information  infrastructure  are  crucial   responsibilities  of the Federal Security Service (FSB) and the  Ministry  of Digital Development, Communications, and Mass Media.  These   organizations  collaborate  closely  with  other   governmental   entities , private  sector   firms , and global  allies  to  bolster  cybersecurity capabilities and tackle  emerging  hazards. These   laws   previously   mentioned  ( Law  on Information, The  Law  on  Sovereign  Internet, and The Federal  Law  on Communications)  have   been   implemented  with the  objective  of  preserving  the  authenticity  and  confidentiality  of  Russia's   digital   environment ,  thereby   exemplifying  the  country's   aspiration  to  ensure  the  protection  of  its  national  interests  and  exercise  authority over the  dissemination  of information. The impact of the  legislation  can be  comprehended   as  a  securitizing   move   that   aims  to  address  the  perceived   threats  to  Russia's  cyber  environment  by  utilizing  the  securitization  theory. The  process  of  securitization   entails  the  construction  of  specific   issues ,  such   as  the  regulation  of information,  as   posing   existential  risks  that  necessitate  exceptional   measures  in the  interest  of  safeguarding  national security. The  legislation  can be  interpreted   through  a  constructivist   lens ,  which   suggests   that  Russia  views  the  digital   realm   as  a  significant  area for the  implementation  of state power and  safeguarding  of national security.  Russia's   legitimization  of the  enactment  of  laws  and  regulations   as   necessary   measures  to  combat   potential   threats  and  maintain  cyber  sovereignty   is   achieved   through  the  securitization  of the cyber domain. Acknowledging  the  varying   interpretations  and  contestations   surrounding   laws   is   crucial   within  the  constructivist  framework.  Critics   contend   that  the  enactment  of the  legislation   could   potentially   hinder  the  exercise  of  freedom  of  expression  and  curtail  the  availability  of information.  These   concerns   pertain  to  differing   interpretations  of security and personal  freedoms , with a focus on the  significance  of an  unimpeded  information  landscape  and  unhampered   exchange  of  ideas . 2.3  Russia's  Cyber  Sovereignty  Strategy: Shaping Global Information Security Russia's   stance  on cyber- sovereignty   is   demonstrated  by  its   proactive   participation  in international information governance, with the  aim  of  influencing  global  norms  and  regulations  in the  digital   sphere .  This   is   exemplified  by  Russia's   acknowledgement  of the  interconnectedness  of the  virtual   realm  and the  significance  of international  cooperation  in  mitigating   shared  cyber  threats .   According  to Alexander  Klimburg , an  expert  in cybersecurity,  Russia's   involvement  in information security on a global scale  indicates   its   acknowledgement   that   individual   states  are  incapable  of  adequately   addressing  cyber  threats   independently . The  statement   embodies  a  constructivist   perspective   that   acknowledges  the  significance  of  communal   norms  and the  necessity  of collaborative  endeavors  to  address   worldwide   predicaments  in the  digital  domain. The  participation  of Russia in global forums,  such   as  the United Nations Group of  Governmental   Experts  on  Developments  in the Field of Information and  Telecommunications  in the  Context  of International Security,  serves   as  a  testament  to  its   dedication  to  collaborating  with  other  countries in the  advancement  of information security. Russia  employs   these   platforms   as  a  means  to  exert   influence  over  worldwide   dialogues  and  mold  standards  that  are  congruent  with  its   domestic   interests .
